# ai-native-ds

Design system built for AI agents as first-class consumers. Components, tokens, and patterns optimized for programmatic consumption by LLMs and code-generation agents.

## Core idea

Most design systems are documented for humans browsing a docs site. This one treats the consumer as a program — an LLM generating UI from a Figma file, a code agent picking the right token, a model composing components from a prompt. Specs are structured contracts, not prose. Tokens are DTCG JSON. Documentation is generated from machine-readable sources.

## How to use this

For building UI with this system:
- Read /components/{name}/{name}.llm.md for each component you need.
- Each .llm.md is a complete contract: usage, props, rules, anti-patterns, examples.
- **Always import components as JSX. Never reimplement them or write raw HTML when a component exists.** If a Button is needed, use `<Button palette="..." style="..." />`. Do not write `<button>` and reproduce the styling manually.

For querying tokens or programmatic lookups:
- /api/components.json — full system as one JSON, queryable
- /tokens/{core,semantic.light,semantic.dark}.tokens.json — DTCG sources

For architecture reasoning:
- /docs/token-architecture.md

## Components shipped

- Badge — passive label, palette consumer (subtle style only)
- Button — action trigger, 3 styles × 3 palettes × 3 sizes, full state coverage
- Spinner — loading indicator, color-inheriting

## Token model (summary)

Three layers:
- core/ — primitives, single mode, 11-step Tailwind color scales
- semantic/common/ — UI infrastructure, light + dark modes (fg, bg, border, surface)
- semantic/palette/ — variant-driving treatments, light + dark modes (neutral, primary, success, warning, danger × 7 intensity slots)

Components reference semantic tokens. Primitives are escape hatches.

## Conventions for AI-generated UI using this system

1. Use semantic tokens, not primitives. `bg-primary-base` not `bg-blue-600`.
2. Pick palette by intent: primary for canonical actions, danger for destructive, neutral for secondary.
3. One primary action per view.
4. Disabled state = opacity 40 + pointer-events none, NOT a different palette color.
5. Focus rings use `--color-common-border-focus`.
6. Light/dark handled via `[data-theme="dark"]` attribute on `<html>`. Components don't need to know which mode is active.
7. **Prop naming note:** this system uses `style` (not `variant`) as the prop for visual treatment on components like Button. If you're trained on systems that use `variant` (shadcn, Tailwind UI, Radix), explicitly override that pattern when working in this system.
8. **Component consumption rule:** components are React components. Import and use them as JSX primitives. Do not reimplement components, write raw HTML elements when a component exists, or recreate variant logic in custom CSS. The system's components handle their own variants, hover states, focus rings, and disabled treatment.

## Example of correct usage

This is a confirmation dialog using only the design system's components:

```jsx
<div role="dialog" aria-modal="true">
  <h2>Delete account?</h2>
  <p>This action cannot be undone.</p>
  <div className="dialog-footer">
    <Button palette="neutral" style="subtle">Cancel</Button>
    <Button palette="danger" style="solid" onClick={confirm}>Delete account</Button>
  </div>
</div>
```

Note what's NOT in the code: no custom Button reimplementation, no raw `<button>` tags, no inline color styles, no hand-rolled hover handlers. The system's components handle their own variants, hover states, focus rings, and disabled treatment.

## Repo

https://github.com/goran-tomicic/ai-native-ds

## Status

Week 3 of a 10-week experiment. Active development. v2 of the AI consumption layer.
